items.title: Regression vs Classification
items.description: Logistic regression is the flagship binary classification algorithm despite its name — it predicts class membership probabilities, not continuous y. Regression vs Classification defines problem type, output heads, and why the regression label refers to log-odds linearity, not a numeric target.

items.title: Loss Functions (BCE)
items.description: Training minimizes binary cross-entropy paired with sigmoid output. Loss Functions is the canonical home for the BCE formula alongside MSE/MAE/CE; this topic owns the sigmoid + BCE algorithm pairing and decision-boundary interpretation.

items.title: Gaussian Naive Bayes
items.description: Both solve classification with probabilistic outputs, but Gaussian NB is generative (models P(x|y) under Gaussian + independence assumptions) while logistic regression is discriminative (models P(y|x) directly via sigmoid). NB can work well on small data; logistic regression needs more examples but makes fewer distributional assumptions on features.

items.title: ROC & AUC
items.description: Logistic regression outputs ranked probabilities ideal for ROC curves and AUC — threshold-free evaluation of separability. ROC & AUC covers TPR/FPR trade-offs; here we produce the ŷ scores those metrics consume.

items.title: Linear Regression
items.description: Linear regression predicts continuous ŷ = Xw + b with MSE; logistic regression shares the linear-in-parameters form but applies sigmoid and BCE for binary labels. Pick the algorithm to match target type, not the shared word regression.
